questions.question: What makes YOLO different from two-stage detectors like Faster R-CNN?

options.A: YOLO uses a deeper network with more layers
options.B: YOLO predicts bounding boxes and classes in a single pass through the network
options.C: YOLO generates region proposals before classifying them
options.D: YOLO only works on high-resolution images

questions.explanation: YOLO (You Only Look Once) treats object detection as a single regression problem, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities in one forward pass. This makes it much faster than two-stage detectors like Faster R-CNN, which first propose regions and then classify them separately.

questions.question: What does IoU (Intersection over Union) measure?

options.A: How many objects were correctly classified in an image
options.B: The overlap between a predicted bounding box and the ground truth box
options.C: The total number of pixels in a segmentation mask
options.D: The confidence score of a detection

questions.explanation: IoU measures how well a predicted bounding box aligns with the ground truth by dividing the area of overlap (intersection) by the total area covered by both boxes (union). An IoU of 1.0 means perfect overlap, while 0.0 means no overlap at all.

questions.question: What is the main difference between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation?

options.A: Semantic segmentation is faster than instance segmentation
options.B: Instance segmentation uses bounding boxes while semantic segmentation does not
options.C: Semantic segmentation labels every pixel by class but cannot tell apart individual objects of the same class
options.D: Instance segmentation only works on images with a single object

questions.explanation: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel (e.g., all car pixels get the same label), but it cannot distinguish between two separate cars side by side. Instance segmentation goes further by assigning both a class label and a unique identity to each individual object.

questions.question: Why is non-maximum suppression (NMS) needed in object detection?

options.A: To increase the number of detections so no object is missed
options.B: To resize bounding boxes to a standard size
options.C: To remove duplicate detections for the same object, keeping only the most confident one
options.D: To convert bounding boxes into segmentation masks

questions.explanation: Object detectors often produce multiple overlapping bounding boxes for the same object. NMS solves this by keeping the highest-confidence detection and removing all other detections that overlap significantly with it. This ensures each object is detected only once.

questions.question: What are anchor boxes in object detection?

options.A: The final output bounding boxes after detection
options.B: Predefined boxes of various sizes and shapes that the model uses as starting references to predict object locations
options.C: Boxes drawn manually by humans to label training data
options.D: Boxes used to crop the image into smaller pieces

questions.explanation: Anchor boxes are predefined reference boxes with different scales and aspect ratios placed across the image. Instead of predicting bounding box coordinates from scratch, the model predicts small adjustments (offsets) relative to these anchors, making the detection task easier to learn.

questions.question: What is special about U-Net's architecture for segmentation?

options.A: It uses only fully connected layers for pixel classification
options.B: It has a symmetric encoder-decoder structure with skip connections that preserve spatial detail
options.C: It processes images one pixel at a time
options.D: It requires millions of training images to work

questions.explanation: U-Net has a U-shaped architecture with an encoder that captures context through downsampling and a decoder that restores spatial resolution. Skip connections concatenate feature maps from the encoder to the decoder at each level, helping the network combine deep semantic understanding with fine spatial detail. This design works well even with small datasets.

questions.question: How does a Vision Transformer (ViT) process an image?

options.A: It applies convolutions to extract features at multiple scales
options.B: It splits the image into patches and processes them as a sequence using self-attention
options.C: It applies attention directly to every individual pixel
options.D: It generates the image one pixel at a time like a language model generates words

questions.explanation: ViT divides an image into fixed-size patches (e.g., 16x16 pixels), treats each patch like a token in a sentence, and processes the sequence of patches through a Transformer encoder using self-attention. Position embeddings are added so the model knows where each patch came from in the original image.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)?

options.A: It reduces the model size by removing unnecessary layers
options.B: It creates feature maps at multiple scales so the model can detect both small and large objects effectively
options.C: It eliminates the need for anchor boxes
options.D: It replaces the backbone CNN entirely

questions.explanation: FPN builds a pyramid of feature maps by combining high-resolution features (good for small objects) with semantically rich low-resolution features (good for understanding what objects are). This multi-scale representation helps the detector find objects of all sizes in an image.

questions.question: What is the purpose of transfer learning in computer vision?

options.A: To train a model from scratch on each new task
options.B: To use a model pretrained on a large dataset and adapt it to a new, often smaller, dataset
options.C: To transfer images from one format to another
options.D: To combine multiple models into a single ensemble

questions.explanation: Transfer learning takes a model that was already trained on a large dataset like ImageNet and adapts it to a new task. Since the pretrained model has already learned useful visual features (edges, textures, shapes), it can achieve good performance on the new task even with limited training data.

questions.question: What do Class Activation Maps (CAMs) show?

options.A: The exact probability score for each class
options.B: Which regions of the input image influenced the model's prediction the most
options.C: The optimal learning rate for training
options.D: How the model's weights change during training

questions.explanation: Class Activation Maps produce heatmaps highlighting which parts of an image were most important for a particular prediction. For example, if a model classifies an image as 'dog,' the CAM would show high activation over the dog region. This helps us interpret and trust the model's decisions.

questions.question: How does the CutMix data augmentation technique differ from Mixup?

options.A: CutMix pastes a rectangular patch from one image onto another and mixes the labels in proportion to the patch area, while Mixup blends two whole images with a weighted average of their pixels and labels
options.B: CutMix randomly changes brightness and contrast, while Mixup mirrors the image horizontally
options.C: CutMix and Mixup both only flip the image, but in different directions
options.D: CutMix is a regularization technique applied to neurons inside the network, while Mixup transforms the input image

questions.explanation: CutMix cuts a rectangular region out of one image and pastes it onto another, mixing the labels by the area each image contributes, whereas Mixup overlays two entire images using a weighted pixel average. Both combine two images into one training example, but CutMix does it spatially and Mixup does it by blending.

questions.question: What does Mask R-CNN add to the Faster R-CNN architecture?

options.A: A region proposal network so it can generate candidate boxes
options.B: A single-pass grid prediction that makes it as fast as YOLO
options.C: A parallel branch that predicts a pixel-level mask for each detected object, enabling instance segmentation
options.D: Position embeddings so it can apply self-attention to image patches

questions.explanation: Mask R-CNN extends Faster R-CNN by adding a mask-prediction branch that outputs a binary mask for each region of interest, on top of the existing class and box outputs. This lets it perform instance segmentation by labeling the individual pixels of each detected object.

questions.question: You have a small target dataset that is very similar to ImageNet. Which transfer learning strategy fits best?

options.A: Train the entire network from scratch with randomly initialized weights
options.B: Delete the pretrained backbone and replace it with a new untrained one
options.C: Fine-tune every layer end-to-end using a high learning rate
options.D: Freeze the pretrained backbone and train only a new classification head

questions.explanation: With little data in a domain similar to the pretraining set, the pretrained features already transfer well, so freezing the backbone and training only the new head avoids overfitting. Fine-tuning all layers on such a small dataset would risk overfitting and erasing useful pretrained knowledge.

questions.question: Why does a Vision Transformer (ViT) add position embeddings to its patch embeddings?

options.A: To reduce the number of patches the model has to process
options.B: Because self-attention is order-agnostic and would otherwise have no information about where each patch came from in the image
options.C: To convert each raw patch into a vector for the first time
options.D: To normalize the pixel values before the patches enter the encoder

questions.explanation: Self-attention treats its inputs as an unordered set, so without position embeddings the model could not tell which patch came from which location. Adding position embeddings injects the spatial position of each patch so the Transformer can reason about layout.

questions.question: What is the role of ROI Pooling in the Faster R-CNN pipeline?

options.A: It converts variable-sized region proposals into fixed-size feature maps so they can be fed to the classification head
options.B: It removes duplicate overlapping detections after classification
options.C: It generates the initial set of region proposals from the feature map
options.D: It measures the overlap between a predicted box and the ground-truth box

questions.explanation: Region proposals come in many different sizes, but the classification head requires fixed-size inputs, so ROI Pooling crops each proposal's region from the feature map and resizes it to a uniform dimension. (Removing duplicates is NMS, generating proposals is the RPN, and measuring overlap is IoU.)
